metadata.title: Build an AI Architecture Autonomy Classifier with Claude
metadata.note: cli-ideas Card 14 — Ch 22 (Shipit: AI Intelligence and Multi-Agent Systems). DOUBLE-CHECK: AutoGPT cost ~$80 and delivered nothing; Cursor keeps control with developer; compounding error: 0.9^10≈35%, 0.9^20≈12%, 0.9^40≈1.5%; four patterns: single call, chained, ReAct, multi-agent. ARCHITECTURAL WARNING for Pattern C (horizon-autonomous) + LOW locatability.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — Claude UI, ask shown answered; IN-FOR-BEAR
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Build it with Claude, then take it apart.

props.command: claude "classify this AI product's architecture — autonomy pattern, locatability rating, agent specifications"
output: ARCHITECTURAL WARNING — insert seam between planner and executor

beats.role_note: stakes — BEFORE CLI loop; SHOW-DON'T-TELL: animate the Cursor vs AutoGPT comparison
beats.narration_text: Why did AutoGPT cost eighty dollars and deliver nothing, while Cursor works reliably enough that professional developers trust it with production code? Same underlying model capability. Opposite architecture decisions. AutoGPT handed decision-making to the AI. Cursor keeps it with the developer. That single choice determines everything downstream: reliability, cost, debuggability, and what the user experiences when something goes wrong. The compounding error math is unforgiving: a ten percent error rate at each step means that after forty steps — well within AutoGPT's range — there is roughly a one-and-a-half percent chance of an error-free result. Architecture is the brand decision. This tool makes it explicit.
beats.visual_intent: Title card: Cursor vs AutoGPT side-by-side — 'same model, opposite architecture'; then compounding error chart showing probability of error-free output vs steps: 100% at step 0, ~35% at step 10, ~12% at step 20, ~1.5% at step 40; no composer yet

beats.role_note: ClaudeComposerAsk — show and discuss the prompt
beats.narration_text: Into Claude. Given this product description, classify which of the four autonomy patterns it instantiates — Pattern A, a prompt wrapper; Pattern B, a ReAct loop; Pattern C, horizon-autonomous; or Pattern D, a specialized pipeline role. Then rate failure locatability: HIGH if failure is isolated to one named component, MEDIUM if investigation needed across two or three, LOW if the system is a mega-agent where failure location is structurally unknowable. For each AI role, write an agent specification with inputs, outputs, permitted actions, forbidden actions, and escalation condition. If locatability is LOW, fire the ARCHITECTURAL WARNING.

props.command:
claude "classify this AI product's architecture:
  [PRODUCT DESCRIPTION]
  Step 1: pattern — A (prompt-wrapper) / B (ReAct loop) /
    C (horizon-autonomous) / D (pipeline role)
  Step 2: failure-locatability — HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
  Step 3: agent spec per role:
    Role | Inputs (typed) | Outputs (typed) |
    Permitted | Forbidden | Escalation
  If LOW: ARCHITECTURAL WARNING —
    recommend seam between [A] and [B]"

beats.role_note: ClaudeCodeBeat — actual classifier script; trimmed to the lines that teach
beats.narration_text: Read it. The agent spec section is the load-bearing part. Role names activate training-data associations in the model — Competitive Intelligence Analyst behaves differently than Agent-One. The forbidden actions field is where you write what the agent cannot do: no writing to external APIs without checkpoint, no spawning sub-agents, no goal modification. Those constraints are the seam. The seam is what makes failures locatable.

props.title: arch_classifier.py
props.code:
# arch_classifier.py  —  Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

SYSTEM = """You are an AI architecture classifier.
Given a product description:
1. Pattern: A(prompt-wrapper)/B(ReAct)/C(horizon-auto)/D(pipeline)
2. Locatability: HIGH(isolated)/MEDIUM(2-3 components)/LOW(mega-agent)
3. Per AI role — agent spec:
   Role | Inputs (typed) | Outputs (typed)
   Permitted actions | Forbidden actions | Escalation
If locatability=LOW:
  print: 'ARCHITECTURAL WARNING: recommend seam between'
  + the two roles that should be separated."""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=2048,
    system=SYSTEM,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sys.stdin.read()}]
)
print(result.content[0].text)

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — mega-agent fires WARNING
beats.narration_text: Run it on a single-model marketing automation system with no separated roles — the classic mega-agent. Pattern C: horizon-autonomous. Locatability: LOW. The ARCHITECTURAL WARNING fires: recommend inserting a seam between the planning role and the execution role. The agent specification shows one role doing everything — inputs unbounded, forbidden actions empty, escalation condition missing. That is the AutoGPT architecture, and the tool names the problem.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec mp4: classifier output printing — 'Pattern: C — horizon-autonomous', 'Locatability: LOW', then red 'ARCHITECTURAL WARNING: recommend seam between Planner and Executor roles'. Single mega-agent spec shown with empty Forbidden actions field highlighted.

beats.narration_text: Now run the same classifier on Cursor's architecture description — a tight augmentation loop where the developer sees every suggestion before it applies. The locatability should flip to HIGH. Then run on the revised mega-agent with a seam inserted — split planning from execution, bound each role's permitted actions, add an escalation condition. Watch the locatability move from LOW to MEDIUM or HIGH.

props.command:
claude "re-classify two architectures:
  1. Cursor: developer-in-loop IDE augmentation,
     tight feedback, every suggestion visible before apply
  2. Revised mega-agent: split into Planner (pattern B) +
     Executor (pattern D), each with bounded permitted
     actions and an escalation condition
  Show how locatability changes for each."

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — locatability improves after seam insertion
beats.narration_text: Cursor: Pattern B, locatability HIGH — failure is isolated to a specific suggestion the developer can immediately reject. Revised mega-agent: Pattern B plus D, locatability MEDIUM — now the failure is traceable to either the planning step or the execution step, not a diffuse tendency of a self-directing system to wander. One seam, one step down the risk ladder. The ARCHITECTURAL WARNING is gone.
beats.visual_intent: Animated three-panel comparison: Cursor (Pattern B, HIGH — green), original mega-agent (Pattern C, LOW — red), revised two-role system (B+D, MEDIUM — yellow); locatability ratings animated as traffic lights; ARCHITECTURAL WARNING present in middle panel, absent in right panel.

beats.narration_text: The architecture choice is the trust decision. The seam placement decides what customers can verify, name, and blame when things go wrong — which decides whether they trust the product at all. AutoGPT was Pattern C with no seams. Cursor is Pattern B with tight checkpoints. Same capability. Same model family. Different brand.
beats.visual_intent: Summary card: 'Architecture = trust decision'; four patterns as a spectrum from bounded to autonomous; locatability ratings alongside; bottom: 'Cursor: B, HIGH trust · AutoGPT: C, LOW trust'

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW — prompt READ aloud AND discussed; greeting 'Your turn.'
beats.narration_text: Your turn. Describe the AI product you are building — what it accesses, what actions it takes without a checkpoint, and what the failure mode looks like from the user's perspective. Paste that into the classifier and read the locatability rating honestly. If it comes back LOW, do not ignore the ARCHITECTURAL WARNING. Adding a seam is almost always a smaller engineering change than recovering from the trust collapse that follows a visible failure. The agent spec the tool generates for each role is the contract between you and the model. Write it down before you wire the nodes.

props.command:
claude "classify my AI product's architecture:
  What it does: [DESCRIPTION]
  External data it accesses: [SOURCES]
  Actions it takes autonomously: [ACTIONS]
  Failure mode from user view: [WHAT USER SEES]
  Give me pattern, locatability, and agent specs"

beats.narration_text: Build an AI Architecture Autonomy Classifier with Claude. This is Liam, in for Bear — build it with Claude, then take it apart.
